---
title: "Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles"
subtitle: "Why the Same Brain Looks Different on Different Screens"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
---

::: {.clinical-vignette}
You're reviewing an EEG with the attending neurologist. The EEG technician asks: "Doctor, would you like the recording displayed in referential or bipolar montage?" The attending says, "Show me the banana." You have no idea what fruit has to do with neurology. But you need to learn — because the same brain activity can look completely different depending on how you display it. And one of those displays makes a seizure source obvious. The other can hide it.
:::

You've learned where the electrodes go (the 10-20 system) and what the frequency bands mean. But there's a piece missing between "recording the signal" and "reading the EEG": **the montage**. This is how EEG signals get wired up for display — and it changes everything about what you see.

## What Is a Montage?

The 10-20 system places electrodes in fixed positions on the scalp — that part doesn't change. A **montage** is the math that happens *after* recording: how each displayed channel is built from the raw electrode voltages.

Here's the fundamental principle: **EEG measures voltage *differences*.** A single electrode carries no meaningful voltage on its own — it needs a second point to compare against. Every channel on an EEG display is always a difference:

$$V_{\text{channel}} = V_{\text{electrode A}} - V_{\text{electrode B}}$$

The montage defines which electrode is A and which is B. Change the pairing, and the same brain activity produces a completely different-looking trace.

::: {.key-concept}
**Analogy**: Measuring elevation. You can express the height of a mountain relative to sea level — one fixed reference for everything — that's a **referential montage**. Or as the slope between two neighboring points along a trail — that's a **bipolar montage**. Same mountain, different description.
:::

## The Five Montages

Clinical EEG is displayed in one of five standard montages. Each is a different linear mapping from the 19 raw electrode voltages to the channels you see on screen:

| Montage | How each channel is built | What it's good at |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Longitudinal bipolar** *(double banana)* | Electrode − neighbor along a **front-to-back** chain | Localizing focal activity via phase reversal — the clinical default |
| **Transverse bipolar** | Electrode − neighbor along a **left-to-right** chain | Catching lateralized or inter-hemispheric asymmetries |
| **Referential (Cz)** | Every electrode − Cz | Amplitude mapping across the scalp (but Cz itself is always 0) |
| **Average reference** | Every electrode − mean of all | "Unbiased" view; standard in quantitative EEG / research |
| **Laplacian** | Electrode − mean of its 4 spatial neighbors | Sharpening focal sources; suppressing diffuse activity |

You'll meet all five in the interactive at the end of this module. But first, let's drill into the clinical default — the **longitudinal bipolar**, nicknamed the **double banana** — because it's what every neurologist defaults to when they sit down to read a study.

## The Double Banana Montage

In a bipolar montage, each channel shows the voltage difference between two *neighboring* electrodes along a chain. The double banana has four chains running from front to back:

| Chain | Electrodes | Derivations |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Left parasagittal | Fp1 → F3 → C3 → P3 → O1 | Fp1-F3, F3-C3, C3-P3, P3-O1 |
| Right parasagittal | Fp2 → F4 → C4 → P4 → O2 | Fp2-F4, F4-C4, C4-P4, P4-O2 |
| Left temporal | Fp1 → F7 → T3 → T5 → O1 | Fp1-F7, F7-T3, T3-T5, T5-O1 |
| Right temporal | Fp2 → F8 → T4 → T6 → O2 | Fp2-F8, F8-T4, T4-T6, T6-O2 |

The name comes from the shape of the chains — two curved lines running front-to-back on each side, like bananas. (Neurologists did not name this.)

Why is this montage the clinical default? Because EEG pathology — seizures, focal slowing, spikes — usually has a *location*. The front-to-back chains let you track where activity is strongest along the **anterior-posterior axis**. If a spike appears in F3-C3 and C3-P3 but not in Fp1-F3 or P3-O1, you know it's centered around C3. If the left temporal chain lights up but the right doesn't, it's lateralized to the left.

::: {.try-it}
**Think about it:** If each chain runs from front to back, what kind of activity would the double banana miss or underestimate? (Hint: what about activity that's purely *lateral* — differences between left and right hemispheres at the same anterior-posterior level?)
:::

## The Dipole: Why EEG Looks the Way It Does

Before we can understand phase reversal properly, we need to understand *what EEG actually measures*. Not just "brain activity" — specifically, the electrical dipoles created by cortical pyramidal neurons.

When a group of pyramidal neurons receives synaptic input, current flows along their long apical dendrites. This creates a tiny **current dipole** — a separation of positive and negative charge, like a miniature battery. One end of the neuron is a current sink (negative), the other is a current source (positive).

A single neuron's dipole is far too small to detect at the scalp. But when **millions of pyramidal neurons** fire in synchrony — all oriented the same way, all producing dipoles at the same time — their fields sum up. That summed dipole is what the EEG electrode detects, after the signal has been smeared and attenuated by cerebrospinal fluid, skull, and scalp.

Two important consequences:

1. **EEG has limited spatial resolution.** You're seeing the summed activity of millions of neurons, blurred through centimeters of tissue. You can't pinpoint activity to a few millimeters — that requires intracranial electrodes.

2. **Dipole orientation matters.** Pyramidal neurons on the crown of a gyrus produce **radial dipoles** that point straight toward the scalp — these are well-detected by nearby electrodes. But neurons on the wall of a sulcus produce **tangential dipoles** that point sideways — these produce a weaker, more spread-out signal, or may be nearly invisible to surface EEG. About two-thirds of the cortical surface is buried in sulci. A lot of brain activity never makes it to the scalp.

::: {.key-concept}
**Why this matters clinically:** Not all seizures show up on scalp EEG. A seizure originating deep in a sulcus might produce tangential dipoles that are invisible to surface electrodes. This is one reason why some epilepsy patients need **intracranial EEG** — electrodes placed directly on or in the brain — to find the seizure focus.
:::

## Phase Reversal: Localizing the Source

::: {.key-concept}
**Clinical convention — negative is up.** Real clinical EEG displays voltage with **negative values pointing upward**. This is the opposite of a normal math plot. It's arbitrary (a 1950s convention), but universal in neurology. The widgets below follow this convention, so "up" means "more negative voltage" — the direction flips your signal-processing intuition by 180°.
:::

Now the payoff. In a bipolar montage, when there's a focal electrical source (a spike, a seizure onset), the two channels that *share* the electrode closest to the source will deflect in **opposite directions**. This is a **phase reversal**.

Why does this happen? Think about the math. Say electrode C3 has the largest negative voltage (it's closest to the source). In the channel F3-C3 you compute (F3 − C3): F3 is less-negative than C3, so F3 − C3 is a positive number. On an EEG display (negative-up), positive values deflect **down**, so F3-C3 deflects down. In the next channel, C3-P3, you compute (C3 − P3): C3 very-negative minus P3 less-negative gives a large negative number — which deflects **up**.

Down in one channel, up in the next. The deflection reverses at C3. That's the phase reversal, and it points you straight at the source.

::: {.key-concept}
Remember Module 1? You shifted two waves by 180 degrees and watched them become perfectly out of phase — when one went up, the other went down. That's exactly what happens at a phase reversal. Same concept, now with real clinical stakes.
:::

Try it yourself. The widget below simulates a bipolar montage with a movable spike source. Select where the spike originates, and watch the phase reversal follow it.

```{=html}
<script src="https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.35.2.min.js"></script>

<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;">
    <label for="sourceSelect" style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.95em;">
      Spike source electrode:
      <select id="sourceSelect" style="padding:6px 12px; border-radius:6px; border:2px solid #2980b9; font-size:0.95em; cursor:pointer;">
        <option value="F3">F3 (frontal)</option>
        <option value="C3" selected>C3 (central)</option>
        <option value="P3">P3 (parietal)</option>
        <option value="O1">O1 (occipital)</option>
      </select>
    </label>
    <span id="sourceNote" style="margin-left:12px; font-size:0.88em; color:#8e44ad; font-style:italic;">
      Phase reversal at C3
    </span>
  </div>

  <div id="phaseReversalPlot" style="width:100%; height:480px;"></div>

  <div id="phaseReversalCaption" style="margin-top:8px; padding:10px; background:#f4ecf7; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid #d2b4de; font-size:0.92em;">
    The spike source is at <b>C3</b>. Notice: Fp1-F3 shows little deflection, <b>F3-C3 deflects DOWN</b>, <b>C3-P3 deflects UP</b>, and P3-O1 shows little deflection. The direction reversal between F3-C3 and C3-P3 is the <b>phase reversal</b> — it tells you C3 is the source.
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  var electrodes = ['Fp1', 'F3', 'C3', 'P3', 'O1'];
  var channels = ['Fp1-F3', 'F3-C3', 'C3-P3', 'P3-O1'];
  var channelColors = ['#3498db', '#2ecc71', '#e74c3c', '#f39c12'];

  function seededRand(s) {
    return function() { s = (s * 16807) % 2147483647; return (s - 1) / 2147483646; };
  }

  function generateEEG(source) {
    var sr = 500, dur = 2;
    var N = sr * dur;
    var rand = seededRand(42);

    // Electrode voltages — background + spike at source
    var spikeIdx = electrodes.indexOf(source);
    var spikeCenter = 1.0; // spike at t=1.0s
    var spikeWidth = 0.03; // 30ms spike

    // Build voltage at each electrode
    var voltages = {};
    electrodes.forEach(function(e, idx) {
      voltages[e] = [];
      for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        var ti = i / sr;
        // Background: mix of alpha + noise
        var bg = 15 * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * 10 * ti + idx * 0.7)
               + 8 * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * 6 * ti + idx * 1.3)
               + 5 * (rand() - 0.5);

        // Spike: Gaussian-windowed sharp transient, negative at source
        var dist = Math.abs(idx - spikeIdx);
        var spikeAmp = 0;
        if (dist <= 2) {
          var falloff = [1.0, 0.4, 0.1][dist];
          var tRel = ti - spikeCenter;
          if (Math.abs(tRel) < 0.15) {
            // Sharp spike followed by slow wave
            var spike = -120 * falloff * Math.exp(-0.5 * Math.pow(tRel / spikeWidth, 2));
            var slowWave = 40 * falloff * Math.exp(-0.5 * Math.pow((tRel - 0.06) / 0.05, 2));
            spikeAmp = spike + slowWave;
          }
        }
        voltages[e].push(bg + spikeAmp);
      }
    });

    // Bipolar derivations
    var traces = [];
    var t = [];
    for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) t.push(i / sr);

    channels.forEach(function(ch, ci) {
      var parts = ch.split('-');
      var e1 = parts[0], e2 = parts[1];
      var y = [];
      for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        y.push(voltages[e1][i] - voltages[e2][i]);
      }
      // Offset for stacking. Negate v for clinical "negative-up" convention.
      var offset = -ci * 120;
      var yOffset = y.map(function(v) { return -v + offset; });
      traces.push({
        x: t, y: yOffset, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
        line: {color: channelColors[ci], width: 1.5},
        name: ch,
        hovertemplate: ch + ': %{text:.0f} uV<extra></extra>',
        text: y.map(function(v) { return v.toFixed(0); })
      });
    });

    return {traces: traces, t: t};
  }

  function updatePlot() {
    var source = document.getElementById('sourceSelect').value;
    var data = generateEEG(source);

    // Find which channels show the phase reversal
    var sIdx = electrodes.indexOf(source);
    var annotations = [];
    var shapes = [];

    // Channel labels on left
    channels.forEach(function(ch, ci) {
      annotations.push({
        x: -0.05, y: -ci * 120,
        xref: 'x', yref: 'y',
        text: '<b>' + ch + '</b>',
        showarrow: false,
        font: {size: 12, color: channelColors[ci]},
        xanchor: 'right'
      });
    });

    // Phase reversal marker
    if (sIdx > 0 && sIdx < electrodes.length - 1) {
      var prChannel1 = sIdx - 1; // channel above
      var prChannel2 = sIdx;     // channel below
      var midY = (-prChannel1 * 120 + -prChannel2 * 120) / 2;
      annotations.push({
        x: 1.0, y: midY,
        xref: 'x', yref: 'y',
        text: '<b>PHASE<br>REVERSAL</b>',
        showarrow: true,
        arrowhead: 2,
        arrowsize: 1.5,
        arrowwidth: 2,
        arrowcolor: '#c0392b',
        ax: 80, ay: 0,
        font: {size: 13, color: '#c0392b', family: 'Arial'},
        bgcolor: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.9)',
        bordercolor: '#c0392b',
        borderwidth: 1,
        borderpad: 4
      });

      // Vertical line at spike time
      shapes.push({
        type: 'line', x0: 1.0, x1: 1.0,
        y0: -prChannel1 * 120 + 80, y1: -prChannel2 * 120 - 80,
        line: {color: 'rgba(192,57,43,0.3)', width: 2, dash: 'dot'}
      });
    } else {
      // Source at the edge — note that phase reversal is incomplete
      annotations.push({
        x: 1.0, y: -1.5 * 120,
        xref: 'x', yref: 'y',
        text: 'Source at chain end<br>(no full phase reversal)',
        showarrow: false,
        font: {size: 11, color: '#e67e22'},
        bgcolor: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.9)',
        bordercolor: '#e67e22',
        borderwidth: 1,
        borderpad: 4
      });
    }

    // Spike time highlight
    shapes.push({
      type: 'rect', x0: 0.85, x1: 1.15,
      y0: 100, y1: -(channels.length - 1) * 120 - 100,
      fillcolor: 'rgba(142,68,173,0.06)', line: {width: 0}
    });

    var layout = {
      xaxis: {title: 'Time (seconds)', range: [0, 2], dtick: 0.5},
      yaxis: {
        showticklabels: false, zeroline: false,
        range: [-(channels.length - 1) * 120 - 100, 120],
        fixedrange: true
      },
      margin: {t: 15, b: 50, l: 85, r: 100},
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      showlegend: false,
      hovermode: 'x unified',
      shapes: shapes,
      annotations: annotations
    };

    Plotly.react('phaseReversalPlot', data.traces, layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});

    // Update caption
    var noteSpan = document.getElementById('sourceNote');
    noteSpan.textContent = 'Phase reversal at ' + source;

    var captionDiv = document.getElementById('phaseReversalCaption');
    if (sIdx > 0 && sIdx < electrodes.length - 1) {
      var ch1 = channels[sIdx - 1];
      var ch2 = channels[sIdx];
      captionDiv.innerHTML = 'The spike source is at <b>' + source + '</b>. Notice: <b>' + ch1 +
        ' deflects DOWN</b> and <b>' + ch2 + ' deflects UP</b>. The direction reversal between ' +
        ch1 + ' and ' + ch2 + ' is the <b>phase reversal</b> — it tells you ' + source + ' is the source.';
      captionDiv.style.background = '#f4ecf7';
      captionDiv.style.borderColor = '#d2b4de';
    } else if (sIdx === 0) {
      captionDiv.innerHTML = 'The spike source is at <b>' + source + '</b> (start of chain). ' +
        'There is no channel above ' + channels[0] + ', so you only see a deflection in one direction. ' +
        'A full phase reversal requires channels on <em>both sides</em> of the source. ' +
        'This is a limitation of bipolar montages at chain endpoints.';
      captionDiv.style.background = '#fef5e7';
      captionDiv.style.borderColor = '#f0c27a';
    } else {
      captionDiv.innerHTML = 'The spike source is at <b>' + source + '</b> (end of chain). ' +
        'There is no channel below ' + channels[channels.length - 1] + ', so you only see a deflection in one direction. ' +
        'End-of-chain sources lack a complete phase reversal — another reason to look at multiple montages.';
      captionDiv.style.background = '#fef5e7';
      captionDiv.style.borderColor = '#f0c27a';
    }
  }

  document.getElementById('sourceSelect').addEventListener('change', updatePlot);
  updatePlot();
})();
</script>
```

::: {.try-it}
**Experiment:**

- Start with the source at **C3** (default). See the phase reversal between F3-C3 and C3-P3? One deflects down, the other deflects up — on a clinical EEG display, "up" means more negative voltage.
- Move the source to **P3**. The phase reversal shifts — now it's between C3-P3 and P3-O1.
- Try **F3** — the phase reversal is between Fp1-F3 and F3-C3.
- Now try **Fp1** or **O1** — what happens? The source is at the *end* of the chain, so there's only one neighboring channel. You get a deflection, but no full reversal. This is a real clinical limitation.
:::

::: {.key-concept}
**Phase reversal summary:** In a bipolar montage, find the two adjacent channels where the deflection flips direction. The electrode they share is closest to the source. This is how neurologists localize spikes and seizure onsets on an EEG — and it's the most important pattern recognition skill for reading a bipolar montage.
:::

## One Dipole, Five Montages — Interactive

Time to revisit all five montages now that you know what phase reversal looks like. Pick a montage, pick a spike source, and watch two things at once: the **stacked channel traces** (what a neurologist sees) and the **scalp topographic map** (the spatial voltage field, re-referenced to match the montage's implied reference).

The underlying dipole doesn't change. What changes is the lens — both the channel layout *and* the zero point the topomap is measured from.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="margin-bottom:0.8em;">
    <div style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.88em; color:#555; margin-bottom:4px;">Montage:</div>
    <div id="montageButtonRow" style="display:flex; flex-wrap:wrap; gap:6px; margin-bottom:10px;"></div>

    <div style="display:flex; flex-wrap:wrap; gap:14px; align-items:center;">
      <label style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.9em;">
        Spike source:
        <select id="montageSourceSelect" style="padding:5px 10px; border-radius:6px; border:2px solid #2980b9; font-size:0.9em; cursor:pointer; margin-left:4px;">
          <option value="Fp1">Fp1</option>
          <option value="F3">F3</option>
          <option value="F7">F7</option>
          <option value="C3" selected>C3</option>
          <option value="T3">T3</option>
          <option value="P3">P3</option>
          <option value="T5">T5</option>
          <option value="O1">O1</option>
          <option value="Cz">Cz</option>
          <option value="Pz">Pz</option>
        </select>
      </label>
      <button id="montagePlayBtn" style="padding:5px 14px; border:2px solid #2980b9; background:#2980b9; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600;">⏸ Pause</button>
      <label style="flex:1; min-width:180px; font-size:0.85em;">
        <span id="montageTimeLabel" style="color:#8e44ad; font-weight:700;">t = 0.00 s</span>
        <input type="range" id="montageScrub" min="0" max="39" step="1" value="0" style="width:100%; accent-color:#8e44ad;">
      </label>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1.3fr; gap:10px; align-items:start;">
    <div id="topomapPlot" style="width:100%; height:380px;"></div>
    <div id="tracesPlot" style="width:100%; height:380px;"></div>
  </div>

  <div id="montageCaption" style="margin-top:10px; padding:10px 12px; background:#f4ecf7; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid #d2b4de; font-size:0.9em; line-height:1.5;">
    Pick a montage and source to begin.
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  const ELEC = [
    {name:'Fp1', x:40, y:14}, {name:'Fp2', x:60, y:14},
    {name:'F7', x:22, y:30}, {name:'F3', x:37, y:30}, {name:'Fz', x:50, y:30}, {name:'F4', x:63, y:30}, {name:'F8', x:78, y:30},
    {name:'T3', x:16, y:50}, {name:'C3', x:34, y:50}, {name:'Cz', x:50, y:50}, {name:'C4', x:66, y:50}, {name:'T4', x:84, y:50},
    {name:'T5', x:22, y:70}, {name:'P3', x:37, y:70}, {name:'Pz', x:50, y:70}, {name:'P4', x:63, y:70}, {name:'T6', x:78, y:70},
    {name:'O1', x:40, y:86}, {name:'O2', x:60, y:86}
  ];
  const EI = {};
  ELEC.forEach(function(e, i) { EI[e.name] = i; });

  function bipolarList(pairs) {
    return pairs.map(function(p) {
      var o = {}; o[p[0]] = 1; o[p[1]] = -1;
      return {name: p[0] + '-' + p[1], weights: o};
    });
  }

  const MONTAGES = {
    longitudinal: {
      label: 'Longitudinal Bipolar (double banana)',
      channels: bipolarList([
        ['Fp1','F3'],['F3','C3'],['C3','P3'],['P3','O1'],
        ['Fp2','F4'],['F4','C4'],['C4','P4'],['P4','O2'],
        ['Fp1','F7'],['F7','T3'],['T3','T5'],['T5','O1'],
        ['Fp2','F8'],['F8','T4'],['T4','T6'],['T6','O2']
      ])
    },
    transverse: {
      label: 'Transverse Bipolar',
      channels: bipolarList([
        ['F7','F3'],['F3','Fz'],['Fz','F4'],['F4','F8'],
        ['T3','C3'],['C3','Cz'],['Cz','C4'],['C4','T4'],
        ['T5','P3'],['P3','Pz'],['Pz','P4'],['P4','T6']
      ])
    },
    referentialCz: {
      label: 'Referential (Cz reference)',
      channels: ELEC.filter(function(e) { return e.name !== 'Cz'; }).map(function(e) {
        var o = {}; o[e.name] = 1; o.Cz = -1;
        return {name: e.name + '-Cz', weights: o};
      })
    },
    averageReference: {
      label: 'Average Reference',
      channels: ELEC.map(function(e) {
        var o = {}, n = ELEC.length;
        ELEC.forEach(function(e2) { o[e2.name] = (e2.name === e.name ? 1 : 0) - 1/n; });
        return {name: e.name + '-avg', weights: o};
      })
    },
    laplacian: {
      label: 'Laplacian (central grid)',
      channels: (function() {
        const NB = {
          F3:['Fz','C3','Fp1','F7'], Fz:['F3','F4','Cz','Fp1'], F4:['Fz','C4','Fp2','F8'],
          C3:['F3','P3','T3','Cz'], Cz:['C3','C4','Fz','Pz'],   C4:['F4','P4','T4','Cz'],
          P3:['C3','O1','T5','Pz'], Pz:['P3','P4','Cz','O1'],   P4:['C4','O2','T6','Pz']
        };
        return Object.keys(NB).map(function(e) {
          var o = {}; o[e] = 1;
          NB[e].forEach(function(n) { o[n] = -0.25; });
          return {name: e + '-Lap', weights: o};
        });
      })()
    }
  };

  const sr = 200, dur = 2, N = sr * dur;
  const TOTAL_FRAMES = 40;
  const DT_FRAME = dur / TOTAL_FRAMES;

  function seededRand(s) { return function() { s=(s*16807)%2147483647; return (s-1)/2147483646; }; }

  function spikeAt(t) {
    const dt = t - 0.8;
    if (dt < -0.02 || dt > 0.22) return 0;
    if (dt < 0.018) return -Math.exp(-(dt*dt)/0.00004);
    return 0.5 * Math.exp(-((dt-0.09)*(dt-0.09))/0.0035);
  }

  function generateElectrodeVoltages(sourceName) {
    const src = ELEC[EI[sourceName]];
    const sigma2 = 25 * 25, spikeAmp = 85;
    const rand = seededRand(42);
    const bgPh = ELEC.map(function() { return rand() * 2 * Math.PI; });
    const bgPh2 = ELEC.map(function() { return rand() * 2 * Math.PI; });

    return ELEC.map(function(e, i) {
      const y = new Array(N);
      const d2 = (e.x - src.x) * (e.x - src.x) + (e.y - src.y) * (e.y - src.y);
      const gain = 1 / (1 + d2 / sigma2);
      for (var j = 0; j < N; j++) {
        const t = j / sr;
        y[j] = 7 * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * 10 * t + bgPh[i])
             + 3 * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * 6 * t + bgPh2[i])
             + spikeAt(t) * spikeAmp * gain;
      }
      return y;
    });
  }

  function applyMontage(V, montage) {
    return montage.channels.map(function(ch) {
      const y = new Array(N).fill(0);
      for (const en in ch.weights) {
        const idx = EI[en];
        if (idx === undefined) continue;
        const w = ch.weights[en];
        for (var j = 0; j < N; j++) y[j] += w * V[idx][j];
      }
      return {name: ch.name, y: y};
    });
  }

  const LAP_NB = {
    F3:['Fz','C3','Fp1','F7'], Fz:['F3','F4','Cz','Fp1'], F4:['Fz','C4','Fp2','F8'],
    C3:['F3','P3','T3','Cz'],  Cz:['C3','C4','Fz','Pz'],  C4:['F4','P4','T4','Cz'],
    P3:['C3','O1','T5','Pz'],  Pz:['P3','P4','Cz','O1'],  P4:['C4','O2','T6','Pz']
  };

  const REF_INFO = {
    longitudinal:     {label: 'avg ref (bipolar has no native topomap)', key: 'avg'},
    transverse:       {label: 'avg ref (bipolar has no native topomap)', key: 'avg'},
    referentialCz:    {label: 'Cz reference', key: 'cz'},
    averageReference: {label: 'average reference', key: 'avg'},
    laplacian:        {label: 'Laplacian (local 2nd derivative)', key: 'lap'}
  };

  function applyReference(V, montageKey) {
    const mode = REF_INFO[montageKey].key;
    const Vref = ELEC.map(function() { return new Array(N); });
    for (var j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      const raw = new Array(ELEC.length);
      for (var i = 0; i < ELEC.length; i++) raw[i] = V[i][j];

      var transformed;
      if (mode === 'cz') {
        const cz = raw[EI.Cz];
        transformed = raw.map(function(v) { return v - cz; });
      } else if (mode === 'lap') {
        var gm = 0;
        for (var k = 0; k < raw.length; k++) gm += raw[k];
        gm /= raw.length;
        transformed = ELEC.map(function(e, i) {
          const nb = LAP_NB[e.name];
          if (!nb) return raw[i] - gm;
          var s = 0;
          for (var k = 0; k < nb.length; k++) s += raw[EI[nb[k]]];
          return raw[i] - s / nb.length;
        });
      } else {
        var m = 0;
        for (var k = 0; k < raw.length; k++) m += raw[k];
        m /= raw.length;
        transformed = raw.map(function(v) { return v - m; });
      }
      for (var i = 0; i < ELEC.length; i++) Vref[i][j] = transformed[i];
    }
    return Vref;
  }

  const GRID = 40;
  const GX = [], GY = [];
  for (var i = 0; i < GRID; i++) { GX.push(5 + 90 * i / (GRID - 1)); GY.push(6 + 88 * i / (GRID - 1)); }

  function inHead(x, y) {
    const dx = (x - 50) / 47, dy = (y - 50) / 44;
    return dx * dx + dy * dy <= 1;
  }

  function topomapGrid(V, sampleIdx) {
    const vAtT = ELEC.map(function(_, i) { return V[i][sampleIdx]; });
    const Z = [];
    for (var iy = 0; iy < GRID; iy++) {
      const row = [];
      for (var ix = 0; ix < GRID; ix++) {
        const xC = GX[ix], yC = GY[iy];
        if (!inHead(xC, yC)) { row.push(null); continue; }
        var num = 0, den = 0, exact = null;
        for (var k = 0; k < ELEC.length; k++) {
          const d2 = (ELEC[k].x - xC) * (ELEC[k].x - xC) + (ELEC[k].y - yC) * (ELEC[k].y - yC);
          if (d2 < 0.6) { exact = vAtT[k]; break; }
          const w = 1 / d2;
          num += w * vAtT[k];
          den += w;
        }
        row.push(exact !== null ? exact : num / den);
      }
      Z.push(row);
    }
    return Z;
  }

  // State
  var currentMontage = 'longitudinal';
  var currentSource = 'C3';
  var currentFrame = 16;
  var playing = true;
  var V = null, Vref = null, channels = null, colorMax = 30, colorMaxField = 30;

  function precompute() {
    V = generateElectrodeVoltages(currentSource);
    channels = applyMontage(V, MONTAGES[currentMontage]);
    Vref = applyReference(V, currentMontage);

    // Fixed field colorMax (from raw V) — stable across non-Laplacian modes so
    // references differ in visible intensity, not just in relative pattern.
    var mF = 0;
    for (var f = 0; f < TOTAL_FRAMES; f++) {
      const si = Math.round(f / TOTAL_FRAMES * N);
      for (var i = 0; i < ELEC.length; i++) {
        const v = Math.abs(V[i][si]); if (v > mF) mF = v;
      }
    }
    colorMaxField = Math.max(10, mF);

    if (REF_INFO[currentMontage].key === 'lap') {
      var mL = 0;
      for (var f = 0; f < TOTAL_FRAMES; f++) {
        const si = Math.round(f / TOTAL_FRAMES * N);
        for (var i = 0; i < ELEC.length; i++) {
          const v = Math.abs(Vref[i][si]); if (v > mL) mL = v;
        }
      }
      colorMax = Math.max(3, mL);
    } else {
      colorMax = colorMaxField;
    }
  }

  function renderTopomap() {
    const sampleIdx = Math.round(currentFrame / TOTAL_FRAMES * N);
    const Z = topomapGrid(Vref, sampleIdx);
    const traces = [
      {
        type: 'contour', z: Z, x: GX, y: GY,
        colorscale: 'RdBu', reversescale: true,
        zmin: -colorMax, zmax: colorMax,
        contours: { showlines: false, coloring: 'fill' },
        hovertemplate: '%{z:.0f} μV<extra></extra>',
        colorbar: { title: { text: 'μV', font: {size: 10} }, thickness: 8, len: 0.7, x: 1.02 }
      },
      (function() {
        const isRefMode = REF_INFO[currentMontage].key === 'cz';
        function label(e) {
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz') return 'Cz (REF=0)';
          return e.name;
        }
        function sz(e) {
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz') return 22;
          return e.name === currentSource ? 20 : 12;
        }
        function fill(e) {
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz' && e.name === currentSource) return '#f39c12';
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz') return '#1abc9c';
          return e.name === currentSource ? '#f39c12' : 'rgba(255,255,255,0.85)';
        }
        function edge(e) {
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz') return '#16a085';
          return e.name === currentSource ? '#c0392b' : '#333';
        }
        function ew(e) {
          if (isRefMode && e.name === 'Cz') return 3;
          return e.name === currentSource ? 2 : 1;
        }
        return {
          type: 'scatter', mode: 'markers+text',
          x: ELEC.map(function(e) { return e.x; }),
          y: ELEC.map(function(e) { return e.y; }),
          text: ELEC.map(label),
          textposition: 'middle center',
          textfont: { size: 8, color: '#222' },
          marker: {
            size: ELEC.map(sz),
            color: ELEC.map(fill),
            line: { color: ELEC.map(edge), width: ELEC.map(ew) }
          },
          hoverinfo: 'text', showlegend: false
        };
      })()
    ];

    const layout = {
      title: { text: 'Scalp topomap — t = ' + (currentFrame * DT_FRAME).toFixed(2) + ' s<br><span style="font-size:0.82em; color:#888;">' + REF_INFO[currentMontage].label + '</span>', font: { size: 12, color: '#555' } },
      xaxis: { range: [0, 100], visible: false, scaleanchor: 'y', scaleratio: 1 },
      yaxis: { range: [100, 0], visible: false },
      margin: { t: 50, b: 10, l: 10, r: 50 },
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      shapes: [
        { type:'circle', xref:'x', yref:'y', x0:3, y0:6, x1:97, y1:94, line:{color:'#222', width:2}, fillcolor:'rgba(0,0,0,0)' },
        { type:'path', path:'M 46,6 L 50,0 L 54,6 Z', line:{color:'#222', width:1.5}, fillcolor:'rgba(0,0,0,0)' }
      ]
    };

    Plotly.react('topomapPlot', traces, layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
  }

  function renderTraces() {
    const nCh = channels.length;
    const t = Array.from({length: N}, function(_, i) { return i / sr; });
    const spacing = 80;
    const traces = channels.map(function(ch, idx) {
      return {
        x: t, y: ch.y.map(function(v) { return -v - idx * spacing; }),
        type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
        line: { color: '#2c3e50', width: 1.1 },
        hoverinfo: 'skip', showlegend: false, name: ch.name
      };
    });

    const cursorT = currentFrame * DT_FRAME;
    const layout = {
      xaxis: { title: 'Time (s)', range: [0, dur], showgrid: false },
      yaxis: {
        range: [-spacing * nCh + 40, spacing * 0.5],
        tickvals: channels.map(function(_, idx) { return -idx * spacing; }),
        ticktext: channels.map(function(ch) { return ch.name; }),
        tickfont: { size: 9 },
        showgrid: false
      },
      shapes: [{
        type: 'line', x0: cursorT, x1: cursorT,
        yref: 'paper', y0: 0, y1: 1,
        line: { color: '#e74c3c', width: 1.5 }
      }],
      margin: { t: 15, b: 38, l: 55, r: 10 },
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      hovermode: false
    };

    Plotly.react('tracesPlot', traces, layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
  }

  function updateCursorOnly() {
    const cursorT = currentFrame * DT_FRAME;
    Plotly.relayout('tracesPlot', {
      'shapes[0].x0': cursorT,
      'shapes[0].x1': cursorT
    });
  }

  function updateCaption() {
    const m = MONTAGES[currentMontage];
    var hook = '';
    if (currentMontage === 'longitudinal') {
      hook = ' Find the two adjacent channels where the deflection flips — their shared electrode is the source.';
    } else if (currentMontage === 'transverse') {
      hook = ' Phase reversals now run left-right instead of front-back. Good for catching lateralized activity that the double banana can miss.';
    } else if (currentMontage === 'referentialCz') {
      hook = ' Every channel is (electrode − Cz). The source electrode shows the biggest deflection. Cz itself is absent — you can\'t subtract it from itself.';
    } else if (currentMontage === 'averageReference') {
      hook = ' Every channel is compared against the mean of all electrodes. Less arbitrary than a single reference, but a strong focal source can contaminate the average.';
    } else if (currentMontage === 'laplacian') {
      hook = ' Each channel is the electrode minus the mean of its four spatial neighbors — a local second derivative. Focal activity stays sharp; diffuse activity cancels out. Edge electrodes (temporal, occipital, frontopolar) fall back to average reference here, since the 4-neighbor Laplacian is only well-defined for the central 9 electrodes.';
    }
    document.getElementById('montageCaption').innerHTML =
      '<b>' + m.label + '</b>, spike at <b>' + currentSource + '</b> (peak near t=0.80 s).' + hook;
  }

  function rebuildButtons() {
    const row = document.getElementById('montageButtonRow');
    const keys = ['longitudinal','transverse','referentialCz','averageReference','laplacian'];
    const colors = {longitudinal:'#2c3e50', transverse:'#1abc9c', referentialCz:'#8e44ad', averageReference:'#e67e22', laplacian:'#e74c3c'};
    row.innerHTML = '';
    keys.forEach(function(k) {
      const btn = document.createElement('button');
      btn.className = 'montage-btn';
      btn.dataset.key = k;
      btn.textContent = MONTAGES[k].label;
      btn.style.cssText = 'padding:6px 14px; border:2px solid ' + colors[k] + '; background:' + colors[k] + '; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600; opacity:' + (k === currentMontage ? '1' : '0.55');
      btn.addEventListener('click', function() {
        currentMontage = k;
        document.querySelectorAll('.montage-btn').forEach(function(b) {
          b.style.opacity = b.dataset.key === k ? '1' : '0.55';
        });
        rebuildAll();
      });
      row.appendChild(btn);
    });
  }

  function rebuildAll() {
    precompute();
    renderTopomap();
    renderTraces();
    updateCaption();
  }

  function tick() {
    if (!playing) return;
    currentFrame = (currentFrame + 1) % TOTAL_FRAMES;
    document.getElementById('montageScrub').value = currentFrame;
    document.getElementById('montageTimeLabel').textContent = 't = ' + (currentFrame * DT_FRAME).toFixed(2) + ' s';
    renderTopomap();
    updateCursorOnly();
    setTimeout(tick, 90);
  }

  document.getElementById('montageSourceSelect').addEventListener('change', function(e) {
    currentSource = e.target.value;
    rebuildAll();
  });
  document.getElementById('montagePlayBtn').addEventListener('click', function() {
    playing = !playing;
    this.textContent = playing ? '⏸ Pause' : '▶ Play';
    if (playing) tick();
  });
  document.getElementById('montageScrub').addEventListener('input', function(e) {
    playing = false;
    document.getElementById('montagePlayBtn').textContent = '▶ Play';
    currentFrame = parseInt(e.target.value);
    document.getElementById('montageTimeLabel').textContent = 't = ' + (currentFrame * DT_FRAME).toFixed(2) + ' s';
    renderTopomap();
    updateCursorOnly();
  });

  rebuildButtons();
  rebuildAll();
  tick();
})();
</script>
```

::: {.key-concept}
**The montage is a lens, not the signal.** The scalp topomap is the ground truth — the actual voltage at every point on the scalp. Every montage you can display is a linear combination of those 19 values. That's why source localization algorithms work on the raw, un-montaged data — any single montage throws information away or reshapes it for the human eye.
:::

## Quiz: Check Your Understanding

```{=html}
<div class="quiz-container">
  <h4>Quiz</h4>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="mq1">
    <p><strong>Q1:</strong> In a bipolar montage, what does a "phase reversal" tell you?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq1" value="a"> A. The signal has been filtered incorrectly
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq1" value="b"> B. The electrode where the phase reversal occurs is closest to the source of the activity
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq1" value="c"> C. The patient moved during the recording
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq1" value="d"> D. Two electrodes have become disconnected
    </label>
    <div id="mq1feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="mq2">
    <p><strong>Q2:</strong> Why can't surface EEG detect activity from tangential dipoles in deep sulci very well?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq2" value="a"> A. The skull blocks tangential electric fields completely
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq2" value="b"> B. Tangential dipoles are too small to measure
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq2" value="c"> C. The dipole is oriented parallel to the scalp surface, producing minimal voltage difference at nearby electrodes
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq2" value="d"> D. Sulcal neurons don't produce dipoles
    </label>
    <div id="mq2feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;" id="mq3">
    <p><strong>Q3:</strong> The double banana montage runs chains of electrodes from front to back. It is best at localizing activity along which axis?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq3" value="a"> A. Left-right (lateral)
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq3" value="b"> B. Anterior-posterior (front to back)
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq3" value="c"> C. Superior-inferior (top to bottom)
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="mq3" value="d"> D. It localizes equally well in all directions
    </label>
    <div id="mq3feedback"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  var answers = {
    mq1: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Filtering changes the signal shape, but phase reversal is a spatial pattern, not a filter artifact. It comes from the voltage distribution across electrodes.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. Phase reversal occurs because the electrode closest to the source has the most extreme voltage. The bipolar channels on either side of it subtract this large value, producing deflections in opposite directions. The shared electrode is your source.'],
      c: ['incorrect', 'Movement artifact typically affects many channels simultaneously and doesn\'t produce the systematic up-then-down pattern of a true phase reversal.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'A disconnected electrode produces artifact in just one channel (electrode pop), not the paired opposite-direction deflections of a phase reversal.']
    }},
    mq2: {correct: 'c', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'The skull attenuates and smears electrical signals, but it doesn\'t block tangential fields completely. The real issue is the dipole orientation relative to the electrode.'],
      b: ['incorrect', 'Tangential dipoles aren\'t necessarily smaller. The issue is geometry — their electric field projects sideways rather than toward the electrode above.'],
      c: ['correct', 'Correct. A tangential dipole\'s positive and negative poles are arranged side-by-side relative to the scalp surface. A nearby electrode "sees" roughly equal contributions from both poles, which cancel out. This is why about two-thirds of the cortical surface (buried in sulci) is relatively invisible to surface EEG.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'Neurons in sulci absolutely produce dipoles — they\'re the same pyramidal neurons as elsewhere in the cortex. The issue is the orientation of those dipoles relative to the scalp electrodes.']
    }},
    mq3: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Left-right localization is possible by comparing the left and right chains, but the bipolar derivations themselves run front-to-back. A transverse montage (ear-to-ear chains) would be better for left-right localization.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. The double banana\'s four chains all run from frontal to occipital. Phase reversals along these chains tell you the anterior-posterior position of the source. For left-right localization, neurologists use a transverse montage — chains running ear-to-ear.'],
      c: ['incorrect', 'EEG electrodes are on the scalp surface — they can\'t distinguish superior-inferior depth easily. That requires intracranial electrodes.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'No montage is equally good in all directions. The double banana is optimized for anterior-posterior localization because that\'s the direction its chains run. Neurologists use multiple montages to localize in different axes.']
    }}
  };

  ['mq1','mq2','mq3'].forEach(function(qid) {
    var radios = document.querySelectorAll('input[name="' + qid + '"]');
    radios.forEach(function(r) {
      r.addEventListener('change', function() {
        var fb = answers[qid].feedback[r.value];
        var div = document.getElementById(qid + 'feedback');
        div.className = fb[0] === 'correct' ? 'quiz-feedback-correct' : 'quiz-feedback-incorrect';
        div.innerHTML = (fb[0] === 'correct' ? '&#10003; ' : '&#10007; ') + fb[1];
      });
    });
  });
})();
</script>
```

## Summary

You now understand the layer between raw electrode recordings and what appears on the screen:

1. **A montage defines how signals are displayed**, not where electrodes go. It's the pairing of electrodes into channels.
2. **The double banana montage** is the clinical workhorse — four front-to-back chains of bipolar derivations, best at anterior-posterior localization.
3. **Cortical dipoles** are the source of EEG signals. Radial dipoles (on gyrus crowns) are well-detected; tangential dipoles (in sulci) may be invisible. This limits what surface EEG can see.
4. **Phase reversal** is the key localization tool in bipolar montages — the electrode where the deflection flips direction is closest to the source.

Next up: not everything on that EEG screen is brain activity. Eye blinks, muscle tension, heartbeats, and loose electrodes all contaminate the recording. Time to learn the difference.

::: {.module-nav}
[← Module 5: EEG Basics](05_eeg_basics.qmd)
[Module 6: Artifacts →](06_artifacts.qmd)
:::
